questions.text: The probability of an event E, P(E), always lies between:
questions.explanation: Probability is a measure of likelihood and is defined in the range [0, 1].

questions.text: The sum of the probabilities of all elementary events of an experiment is:
questions.explanation: One of the fundamental properties of probability is that the sum of probabilities of all mutually exclusive and exhaustive events is 1.

questions.text: If P(E) = 0.05, what is the probability of 'not E'?
questions.explanation: P(E) + P(not E) = 1. Therefore, P(not E) = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95.

questions.text: The probability of an impossible event is:
questions.explanation: An event that cannot happen has zero favorable outcomes, hence its probability is 0.

questions.text: The probability of a sure event is:
questions.explanation: A sure event is certain to happen, meaning all possible outcomes are favorable, so P(E) = 1.

questions.text: Which of the following cannot be the probability of an event?
questions.explanation: Probability cannot be negative or greater than 1. -1.5 is outside the valid range [0, 1].

questions.text: A die is thrown once. The probability of getting a prime number is:
questions.explanation: Possible outcomes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Prime numbers: 2, 3, 5. P = 3/6 = 1/2.

questions.text: A die is thrown once. The probability of getting a number lying between 2 and 6 is:
questions.explanation: Numbers between 2 and 6 are 3, 4, 5. There are 3 such numbers. P = 3/6 = 1/2.

questions.text: A coin is tossed two times. The total number of possible outcomes is:
questions.explanation: Outcomes are HH, HT, TH, TT. Total = 2^2 = 4.

questions.text: If two coins are tossed simultaneously, the probability of getting at least one head is:
questions.explanation: Outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT. Favorable (at least one H): HH, HT, TH. P = 3/4.

questions.text: A bag contains 3 red balls and 5 black balls. A ball is drawn at random. The probability that the ball is red is:
questions.explanation: Total balls = 3 + 5 = 8. Red balls = 3. P = 3/8.

questions.text: In a deck of 52 playing cards, the probability of drawing a red face card is:
questions.explanation: Face cards: J, Q, K of 4 suits = 12. Red face cards: 2 suits (Hearts, Diamonds) * 3 = 6. P = 6/52 = 3/26.

questions.text: The probability of getting a non-leap year having 53 Sundays is:
questions.explanation: A non-leap year has 365 days = 52 weeks + 1 day. The extra day can be any of the 7 days. P(Sunday) = 1/7.

questions.text: The probability of getting 53 Sundays in a leap year is:
questions.explanation: A leap year has 366 days = 52 weeks + 2 days. Pairs of extra days: (S,M), (M,T), (T,W), (W,Th), (Th,F), (F,Sa), (Sa,S). 2 pairs contain Sunday. P = 2/7.

questions.text: A box contains cards numbered from 1 to 20. A card is drawn at random. The probability that the number is a multiple of 3 is:
One card is drawn from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. The probability of getting a king of red colour is:
questions.explanation: There are two red kings: King of Hearts and King of Diamonds. P = 2/52 = 1/26.

questions.text: A letter is chosen at random from the word 'MATHEMATICS'. The probability that it is a vowel is:
questions.explanation: Vowels in MATHEMATICS: A, E, A, I. Total letters = 11. Count = 4. P = 4/11.

questions.text: Two dice are thrown together. The probability that the sum of numbers appearing on the dice is 7 is:
If two dice are thrown together, the probability of getting a doublet is:
The probability of drawing an Ace from a deck of 52 cards is:
questions.explanation: There are 4 Aces in a deck. P = 4/52 = 1/13.

questions.text: A child has a die whose six faces show the letters: A, B, C, D, E, A. The probability of getting 'A' is:
questions.explanation: Letter 'A' appears on 2 faces out of 6. P = 2/6 = 1/3.

questions.text: A box contains 90 discs numbered 1 to 90. If one disc is drawn at random, the probability that it bears a two-digit number is:
questions.explanation: One-digit numbers are 1 to 9 (9 total). Two-digit numbers = 90 - 9 = 81. P = 81/90 = 9/10.

questions.text: In a box of 90 discs numbered 1 to 90, the probability of drawing a perfect square number is:
The probability of getting at most one head when two coins are tossed is:
questions.explanation: Outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT. 'At most one head' means 0 or 1 head: TT, HT, TH. P = 3/4.

questions.text: A card is selected from 52 cards. The probability that it is a spade is:
questions.explanation: There are 13 spades in 52 cards. P = 13/52 = 1/4.

questions.text: A lot consists of 144 ball pens of which 20 are defective. If you buy a pen only if it is good, the probability that you will buy it is:
Two dice are thrown simultaneously. The probability that the product of numbers on the top faces is 6 is:
Game of chance: spinning arrow points at 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8. What is the probability that it will point at a number greater than 2?
questions.explanation: Numbers greater than 2: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8. Count = 6. P = 6/8 = 3/4.

questions.text: If P(E) = 0.65, then P(not E) is:
The probability that a number selected from numbers 1 to 15 is a prime number is:
One card is drawn from a deck of 52 cards. The probability that it is a face card is:
questions.explanation: Face cards: 4 suits * 3 (J, Q, K) = 12. P = 12/52 = 3/13.

questions.text: The probability of getting a doublet of even numbers when two dice are thrown is:
A bag contains 5 red balls and some blue balls. If the probability of drawing a blue ball is double that of a red ball, find the number of blue balls.
questions.explanation: P(Blue) = 2 * P(Red). Let blue balls be x. x/(x+5) = 2 * 5/(x+5) => x = 10.

questions.text: Three coins are tossed simultaneously. The total number of outcomes is:
questions.explanation: For each coin, there are 2 outcomes. For 3 coins, total = 2 * 2 * 2 = 8.

questions.text: If three coins are tossed simultaneously, what is the probability of getting exactly two heads?
questions.explanation: Outcomes: HHH, HHT, HTH, THH, HTT, THT, TTH, TTT. Exactly two heads: HHT, HTH, THH. P = 3/8.

questions.text: If P(E) represents the probability of an event E, then:
questions.explanation: Probability is always between 0 and 1 inclusive.

questions.text: A card is drawn from a deck of 52 cards. The probability that it is a black queen is:
questions.explanation: Black queens are Queen of Spades and Queen of Clubs. P = 2/52 = 1/26.

questions.text: The probability of getting a number divisible by 3 on throwing a die is:
questions.explanation: Numbers divisible by 3 on a die: 3, 6. Count = 2. P = 2/6 = 1/3.

questions.text: Two coins are tossed. The probability of getting at most 2 heads is:
questions.explanation: Outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT. All have 0, 1, or 2 heads. P = 4/4 = 1.

questions.text: The complement of an event E is 'not E'. The value of P(E) + P(not E) is always:
questions.explanation: The sum of the probability of an event and its complement is always 1.
